sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A lab studied Protein Q, a 60-kDa cytosolic enzyme. A mutant allele produced a protein that migrated at 60 kDa on SDS-PAGE but had only 25% of normal catalytic activity. Native PAGE suggested altered quaternary assembly, and size-exclusion chromatography showed that wild-type Protein Q formed a tetramer while the mutant was mostly monomeric. Western blot signal intensity was similar for wild type and mutant samples.
questions.stem: What does similar Western blot signal indicate?

choices.C: Wild-type and mutant Protein Q have similar abundance
choices.A: The mutant has a much larger molecular weight
choices.B: The mutant has normal catalytic activity
choices.D: The mutant cannot bind antibody at all

explanations.C: Correct. Western blot signal intensity is similar, indicating comparable protein abundance in the samples.
explanations.A: SDS-PAGE migration is described as the same 60 kDa, not much larger.
explanations.B: Catalytic activity is reduced despite similar abundance.
explanations.D: Antibody binding must occur for a Western signal to be detected.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Relative Western blot signal, catalytic activity, and tetramer fraction for wild-type and mutant Protein Q.

questions.stem: Why can the mutant have normal SDS-PAGE size but low activity?

choices.B: The mutation may disrupt higher-order assembly without changing polypeptide length
choices.C: The mutant must lack all amino acids
choices.D: Western blotting always proves correct folding

explanations.B: Correct. SDS-PAGE under denaturing conditions mainly reports polypeptide size. The mutant can have the same size but impaired tetramer formation and reduced activity.
explanations.A: SDS-PAGE measures migration by size, not catalytic rate.
explanations.C: The protein still migrates at 60 kDa, so it does not lack all amino acids.
explanations.D: Western blotting detects protein, not necessarily correct folding or assembly.

questions.stem: Which method most directly supports altered quaternary structure?

choices.D: Size-exclusion chromatography showing monomer instead of tetramer

explanations.D: Correct. Size-exclusion chromatography separates proteins by hydrodynamic size and can distinguish monomers from tetramers under native conditions.
explanations.A: A codon table does not directly measure protein assembly.
explanations.B: A glucose tolerance test assesses endocrine/metabolic physiology.
explanations.C: A Gram stain identifies bacterial cell wall properties.

questions.stem: What level of protein structure is most affected by loss of tetramer formation?

explanations.A: Correct. Quaternary structure refers to interactions among multiple polypeptide subunits. A tetramer-to-monomer shift is a quaternary-structure defect.
explanations.B: Primary structure is the amino acid sequence; the key observation is assembly.
explanations.C: Peptide bonds remain present in the monomeric protein.
explanations.D: The genetic code is not a level of protein structure.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the data?

choices.D: Protein Q activity depends partly on tetramer assembly
choices.A: Protein Q is absent in mutant cells
choices.B: SDS-PAGE proves the mutant is fully functional

explanations.D: Correct. The mutant protein is present at similar abundance and size but has reduced tetramer fraction and activity. This supports the idea that oligomerization is important for function.
explanations.A: Western signal shows Protein Q is present.
explanations.B: SDS-PAGE size alone does not prove function.
explanations.C: Protein abundance is similar, arguing against transcription as the primary defect.

questions.passage: Students purified Enzyme R from liver homogenate using ammonium sulfate precipitation, ion-exchange chromatography, and affinity chromatography. Total protein decreased at each step, but specific activity increased from 2 to 25 to 180 to 520 units/mg. The final fraction had lower total activity than the starting homogenate but much higher purity. The instructor emphasized that purification often sacrifices yield to improve specific activity.
questions.stem: Which purification step produced the highest specific activity?

explanations.C: Correct. The affinity fraction has the highest specific activity at 520 units/mg, indicating the greatest enrichment of active Enzyme R per mg protein.
explanations.A: The homogenate has the lowest specific activity.
explanations.B: The salt cut improves activity but not the most.
explanations.D: Ion exchange is intermediate compared with affinity chromatography.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Total protein and specific activity across sequential Enzyme R purification steps.

questions.stem: What does increasing specific activity generally indicate?

choices.B: The enzyme is becoming more purified relative to total protein
choices.A: The enzyme is becoming less abundant in every possible sense
choices.C: All contaminating proteins have higher activity
choices.D: The assay is no longer measuring enzyme function

explanations.B: Correct. Specific activity is activity per mg protein. As contaminants are removed, the target enzyme represents a larger fraction of total protein, increasing specific activity.
explanations.A: Total protein may fall, but the enzyme can be enriched in the remaining fraction.
explanations.C: Contaminants are being removed, not necessarily becoming more active.
explanations.D: The activity assay is still central to determining specific activity.

questions.stem: Why can total activity fall during purification?

choices.A: Some enzyme is lost during handling or fractionation
choices.D: Affinity chromatography converts protein into DNA

explanations.A: Correct. Purification steps can lose some target enzyme even while removing many contaminants. Thus yield may fall as purity rises.
explanations.B: Purification enriches existing enzyme rather than creating it from nothing.
explanations.C: Specific activity increased in the data.
explanations.D: Chromatography separates biomolecules; it does not convert protein into DNA.

questions.stem: What is the likely purpose of affinity chromatography in this purification?

choices.C: To selectively bind Enzyme R or a tagged form using specific interactions

explanations.C: Correct. Affinity chromatography exploits specific binding between the target protein and a ligand, antibody, or tag, giving high purification.
explanations.A: Denaturation is not the purpose of affinity purification.
explanations.B: Genome sequencing is unrelated to enzyme purification.
explanations.D: Blood pressure measurement is physiological, not chromatographic.

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the tradeoff shown in Figure 2?

choices.B: Purity increases as total protein and likely yield decrease
choices.A: Every purification step increases total protein
choices.C: Higher specific activity means lower purity
choices.D: The starting homogenate is the purest fraction

explanations.B: Correct. The figure shows falling total protein but rising specific activity, illustrating the common tradeoff of improved purity with loss of material or yield.
explanations.A: Total protein decreases at each step.
explanations.C: Higher specific activity generally indicates higher purity.
explanations.D: The homogenate has the lowest specific activity and is least pure.
